title: Bayesian Conjugate Update: Normal-Normal
intuition: The posterior mean is a precision-weighted average of the prior mean and the data mean. More data (large n) pulls the posterior toward x̄; strong prior (small τ²) keeps it near μ₀.
pitfalls: σ² is assumed known here. If unknown, a Normal-InverseGamma conjugate is needed.
references: Gelman, A. et al. (2013). Bayesian Data Analysis. 3rd ed. CRC Press.

derivationSteps.explanation: By Bayes' theorem, the posterior is proportional to likelihood × prior. We drop the normalising constant p(x) as it does not depend on μ.
The likelihood for n i.i.d. normals factors, and after dropping constants in x, depends on μ only through (x̄-μ)². The prior gives a quadratic in (μ-μ₀).
Combining both exponentials into a single exp of a sum.
Expanding and collecting terms in μ: the coefficient of μ² is n/σ² + 1/τ² = τₙ⁻². The coefficient of μ is -2(nx̄/σ² + μ₀/τ²) = -2τₙ⁻²μₙ. C is a constant in μ.
hints: Coefficient of -2μ: nx̄/σ² + μ₀/τ², so μₙ = (nx̄/σ² + μ₀/τ²)/τₙ⁻²

derivationSteps.explanation: The posterior is proportional to a normal kernel in μ with mean μₙ and variance τₙ². The constant C contributes only to the normalising constant.
By uniqueness of the normal distribution, the posterior is N(μₙ, τₙ²). The posterior precision τₙ⁻² equals prior precision plus data precision n/σ².

title: OLS Estimator in Linear Regression
statement: \text{In the model } \mathbf{y} = X\boldsymbol{\beta} + \boldsymbol{\varepsilon}, \text{ the OLS estimator is } \hat{\boldsymbol{\beta}} = (X^{\top}X)^{-1}X^{\top}\mathbf{y}
assumptions: X \in \mathbb{R}^{n \times p} \text{ has full column rank}
intuition: OLS minimises the sum of squared residuals. The solution is found by setting the gradient to zero — the 'normal equations' — and solving for β.
pitfalls: (XᵀX) must be invertible — requires X to have full column rank (no multicollinearity).
OLS is BLUE under Gauss-Markov but requires additional assumptions for inference.
references: Greene, W. H. (2018). Econometric Analysis. 8th ed.

derivationSteps.explanation: We minimise the residual sum of squares — the squared Euclidean distance between y and the fitted values Xβ.
Expanding (y-Xβ)ᵀ(y-Xβ) using distributivity of matrix transpose and the scalar identity xᵀy = yᵀx.
Using matrix calculus: ∂(βᵀAβ)/∂β = 2Aβ (for symmetric A=XᵀX) and ∂(bᵀβ)/∂β = b. Setting gradient to zero.
hints: XᵀX is symmetric positive definite when X has full rank.

derivationSteps.explanation: Rearranging the first-order condition gives the normal equations. Any solution minimises RSS.
Since XᵀX is invertible (X has full column rank), premultiplying both sides by (XᵀX)⁻¹ gives the unique OLS estimator.
assumptions: X has full column rank so (XᵀX) is invertible
